coredata.dc:title: l-valine functionalized nitrogen doped non-graphitic carbon dots as a photoluminescent probe for detection of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Bisht, Krishanan
Dutta, Raj Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Here we present l-valine functionalized non-graphitic carbon dots (denoted as N-CDvaline) as a photoluminescent (PL) probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of tetracycline antibiotic. The probe, synthesized by heating of ethylene glycol with l-valine at 170 °C for 10 min, exhibited bright bluish photoluminescence at λemission = 445 nm by exciting at λexcitation = 370 nm with 11.4% photoluminescence quantum yield. The higher resolution transmission electron micrograph, selected area electron diffraction, Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of N-CDvaline suggested highly ordered arrangement of linear transverse chains of oligomers of ethylene glycol and l-valine, attributable to self-assembly. Under optimized conditions, the probe exhibited linear PL quenching by tetracycline in the concentration range between 0.25 and 5.00 μM and satisfied the Stern-Volmer equation, with a detection limit of 0.054 μM. This method is highly selective towards tetracycline detection against host of interfering agents e.g., some common cations, anions and other classes of antibiotics. The PL quenching mechanism is attributed to ground state complex formation between the probe and tetracycline, corroborated by time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy studies and due to inner filter effect (IFE) owing to strong spectral overlap between UV–vis absorption spectrum of tetracycline with excitation and emission spectra of N-CDvaline. The scope for using this method for detection of tetracycline in real samples e.g., river water and milk is successfully demonstrated by spike analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon dots as PL probe
PL quenching by tetracycline
Limit of detection (LOD)
PL lifetime spectroscopy of probe + analyte
Spike analysis of tetracycline in real samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDvaline)
Optimization of the probe (N-CDvaline) synthesis for tetracycline detection
Detection of tetracycline by N-CDvaline as PL probe
Spike analysis of tetracycline in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–b) High-resolution transmission electron micrograph (HRTEM) of N-CDvaline with different magnifications; selected area fast fourier transform (FFT) image at 10 nm scale (inset of 1b) (c) regular arrangements if carbonaceous transverse chain in N-CDvaline.
FT-IR spectra of the synthesized N- CDvaline.
X-ray photoelectron spectra showing (a) survey scan; detailed binding energy spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) N 1s of N-CDvaline.
(a) UV–Vis; (b) PL spectra of N-CDvaline, with the inset showing bluish PL emission at λ = 365 nm under UV light; and (c) PL lifetime spectra of N-CDvaline.

(a) Stern-Volmer plot of N-CDvaline treated with different concentrations of TC; Inset: linear part of Stern−Volmer quenching profile for N-CDvaline treated with TC concentration ranging between 0.25 μM and 5.00 μM; (b) Shows decrease in the PL intensity of N-CDvaline with increase of TC (μM) ranging between 0.25 μM and 250 μM.

(a) Stern-Volmer plot for the probe treated with TC spiked deionized water, municipality tap water and river water samples; (b) Showing decrease in the PL intensity of N-CDvaline in TC spiked milk sample with increasing concentrations of TC ranging between 5 μM and 100 μM; (c) Stern-Volmer plot for the probe with TC spiked milk samples.

PL spectra of the N-CDvaline treated with interfering (a) cations (b) anions and (c) antibiotics; PL quenching response of N-CDvaline probe (d) treated with different interfering cations (50 μM) in the absence and presence of TC, (e) treated with different interfering anions (50 μM) (f) treated with different interfering antibiotics (50 μM).

(a) UV–vis spectra of pristine N-CDvaline and after treating with different concentrations of TC; (b) UV–Vis spectra (dotted blue line) of TC excitation fluorescence spectrum (solid pink line) and PL spectrum (black line) of N-CDvaline.
Represents a probable structure of N-CDvaline formed by laterally connected transverse carbon chains of ethylene glycol oligomer.
Proposed mechanism of formation of transverse carbon chain by oligomerization of ethylene glycol and N-valine.
Data representing recovery analysis of TC concentrations, spiked in municipality Tap water and river water.
Data representing recovery analysis of TC concentrations, spiked in pre-treated Milk samples.
Individual photoluminescence lifetime parameters (τ1, τ2 and τ3) and τavg value of probe (N-CDvaline), probe in presence of 10 μM, 30 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM and 200 μM of tetracycline.
Comparison of LODs for TC detection of Some Recently Published Data Using Different materials with the Present Study.

row.entry: Carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers
Europium doped boron nitride quantum dot
0.019 μM for TC 0.104 μM for OTC
fluorine and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots
Valine functionalized N doped carbon dot(N-CDvaline)
Ground state complex formation with inner filter effect

head.ce:title: l-valine functionalized nitrogen doped non-graphitic carbon dots as a photoluminescent probe for detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, 247667, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, 247667, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we present l-valine functionalized non-graphitic carbon dots (denoted as N-CDvaline) as a photoluminescent (PL) probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of tetracycline antibiotic. The probe, synthesized by heating of ethylene glycol with l-valine at 170 °C for 10 min, exhibited bright bluish photoluminescence at λemission = 445 nm by exciting at λexcitation = 370 nm with 11.4% photoluminescence quantum yield. The higher resolution transmission electron micrograph, selected area electron diffraction, Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of N-CDvaline suggested highly ordered arrangement of linear transverse chains of oligomers of ethylene glycol and l-valine, attributable to self-assembly. Under optimized conditions, the probe exhibited linear PL quenching by tetracycline in the concentration range between 0.25 and 5.00 μM and satisfied the Stern-Volmer equation, with a detection limit of 0.054 μM. This method is highly selective towards tetracycline detection against host of interfering agents e.g., some common cations, anions and other classes of antibiotics. The PL quenching mechanism is attributed to ground state complex formation between the probe and tetracycline, corroborated by time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy studies and due to inner filter effect (IFE) owing to strong spectral overlap between UV–vis absorption spectrum of tetracycline with excitation and emission spectra of N-CDvaline. The scope for using this method for detection of tetracycline in real samples e.g., river water and milk is successfully demonstrated by spike analysis.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen doped carbon dots as PL probe
PL lifetime spectroscopy of probe + analyte
Spike analysis of tetracycline in real samples

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the last decade or so, reports on wide range of residual antibiotics in water bodies and in drinking water has caused a great concern as they are harmful to human health [1–4]. The existing strategies known for water treatment are insufficient for removing these emerging contaminants and hence, they pose threat to ecosystem owing to their pathways into plants, aquatic species and eventually into the human food chain [5–7]. In view of this, rapid and facile analytical methodology is needed for on-site analysis of residual antibiotics in different sample matrices. The concentrations of residual antibiotics in these types of sample matrices are usually of the order of micromolar levels which demand a sensitive and selective detection method. Conventionally, concentrations of such levels are determined by sophisticated instrumental techniques, e.g., high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography coupled to mass spectrometers (LC-MS or LC-MS-MS), capillary electrophoresis (CE), colorimetric method, surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [8–11]. The above mentioned methods mostly require expensive instruments which are not convenient for on-site analysis. In view of this, water soluble photoluminescent nanomaterials have emerged as an excellent alternate probe for detecting wide range of analytes [12–15].

Compared to inorganic quantum dots comprising of toxic heavy metals, the photoluminescent properties of non-toxic water soluble carbogenic nanoparticles, e.g., carbon dots, carbon quantum dots and graphene quantum dots have emerged as alternate photoluminescent probes for the detection of wide range of analytes [16,17]. The major advantages of carbogenic nanoparticles are attributed to their ease of synthesis and functionalization, water solubility, biocompatibility, less toxicity, and high quantum yield without affecting chemical stability and photostability [18,19]. The sensing abilities of these probes are either due to photoluminescence turn-ON or due to Turn-OFF mechanism [20]. The photoluminescence property of these carbogenic nanoparticles is primarily attributed to formation of organic fluorophores that are formed during their synthesis and also depends on other factors, e.g., structure of the carbonaceous core, impurities and heteroatom dopants, functionalization with polar groups [21,22]. Heteroatom doping in carbon dots or carbon quantum dots usually exhibit enhanced PL. Most of these studies are based on nitrogen as dopant as its atomic size is comparable to that of carbon, and it can inject more electrons into the carbon dot structure owing to more valence electrons than carbon [23,24]. A comparable atomic size of dopants is necessary as they impart lesser strain on the structure of the carbon dot, which is an important criterion for improved PL quantum yield. The structure of the carbogenic nanoparticles depends on the choice of carbon precursors and method of synthesis, e.g., a hydrothermal method, a microwave-assisted method, pyrolysis, and by electrochemical oxidation of precursors [25–28]. Mostly, the carbon dots or carbon quantum dots comprise of aromatic rings that are either precursors or they are formed by cyclization during condensation reaction in synthesis step [29,30]. In these cases, the core structure of the carbon dots is made of pure sp2 hybridized carbon or a mix of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon [31].

Though there are a few studies, but lately crystalline carbonaceous nanoparticles with predominantly sp3 hybridized carbon are being developed as PL probes for sensing applications [13,32,33]. The highly ordered carbonaceous nanomaterial could be an advantage for improved sensing applications based on PL Turn-Off mechanism owing to favorable delocalization of photoexcited charge carriers for interaction with analyte. Here, we present a strategy for synthesis of such crystalline N-doped carbon dots using ethylene glycol and l-valine as precursors. l-valine is chosen for two purposes, i.e., (a) for providing acidic proton for oligomerization of ethylene glycol to form carbonaceous chains made of sp3 carbon; (b) to incorporate N-dopant in the carbon structure via its structural amine group. The supply of electrons by the more electronegative nitrogen group is expected to be more due to the inductive effect of two methyl groups in each l-valine unit. An analytical methodology has been developed here for sensing of tetracycline in aqueous medium by the as-synthesized batch of l-valine functionalized carbon dots as a probe via PL quenching mechanism. It has been aimed to achieve detection limits less than the minimum residual levels (MRLs) of tetracycline in drinking water and in food, e.g., 100 ng/mL (i.e., 225 nM) specified by European Union (EU) and 300 ng/mL (676 nM) in milk specified by U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [34]. The laboratory optimized method has been extended to selective detection of tetracycline in real samples, e.g., tap water, river water and milk samples.

l-valine (99%), ethylene glycol (99%) and tetracycline hydrochloride were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. (SRL) India. Hydrochloric acid (35%, v/v) and sodium hydroxide (98%, w/v), were obtained from Himedia Pvt. Ltd, India. ICP-MS standard solution of metal ions, e.g.; Na+, K+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Hg2+ and sodium salts of chloride, fluoride, nitrate, arsenate, sulfate, carbonate, bicarbonate, acetate were procured from Merck, India. All the chemicals are commercially available and used without further purification. A wide range of antibiotics viz., erythromycin, amoxicillin, norfloxacin, penicillin, ciprofloxacin, chloramphenicol and oxytetracycline, were used for selectivity studies. De-ionized water of resistivity 18 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDvaline)
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen doped carbon dots were synthesized by one pot thermal decomposition method by heating 0.3 g of l-valine (2.56 mmol) with 10 mL of ethylene glycol (178.8 mmol) in a 50 mL round bottom flask kept at 170 °C in an oil bath for 10 min under constant magnetic stirring. The color of the solution changed gradually from colorless transparent to light yellow and finally to yellowish brown. The round bottom flask was than cooled to room temperature and the obtained yellowish brown solution of the optimized batch was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 5 min and was purified through a dialysis tube (Serva Visking 8/32, MW 12000) for 28 h. On exposure by UV light of wavelength 365 nm, the solution containing carbon dots functionalized with l-valine exhibited bright blue photoluminescence.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the purified carbon dots were measured on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on Hitachi F-4600 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp. All excitation and emission spectra were recorded using 5 nm slit-width. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were recorded on HORIBA Jobin Yvon “Fluorocube Fluorescence Lifetime System” using NanoLEDs as an excitation source and a TBX-04-D detector. Laser pulses of wavelength λ = 295 nm with a pulse repetition rate of 500 kHz was used as an excitation source. The PL decay curves were fitted using IBH software (DAS6). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy images (HRTEM) of carbon dots were recorded in JEOL JEM-3200FS microscope by operating at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. The sample for HRTEM measurement was prepared by drop casting a suitably diluted solution of purified nitrogen doped carbon dots on a copper grid (Icon Analytical Equipment Pvt. Ltd., India), and dried overnight at 40 °C. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the nitrogen doped carbon dots were recorded on Thermo NICOLET 6700 FT-IR by pelletizing with anhydrous KBr powder (Sigma Aldrich, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded on a PHI 5000 Versa Probe (III) using a multi-technique surface analysis system using a monochromatic Al Kα (1486.7 eV) as X-ray excitation source. 1H and 13C NMR spectra of the carbon dots were recorded ona Bruker ADVANCED 500.13 MHz spectrometer in DMSO-D6 solvent using TMS (tetramethylsilane) as an internal standard. MS-QToF measurements were performed on a (XeVO G2-XS QToF), using electrospray ionization (ESI) in positive mode.

An acidic solution of quinine sulfate (prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4) was used as reference to determine the PLQY of the purified nitrogen doped carbon dots and the absorbance was kept in the range 0.01–0.1 to minimize the Inner filter effect/re-absorption effects. The PLQY measurements were recorded at an excitation wavelength of λex = 370 nm, and it (
Φ

represent the refractive indices of N-CDvaline and quinine sulfate prepared in an aqueous medium, which are taken as 1.330 as both N-CDvaline and quinine sulfate solutions were prepared in deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the probe (N-CDvaline) synthesis for tetracycline detection
ce:section.ce:para: The heating time for preparing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDvaline) as probe was optimized using the photoluminescence quenching response of the probe by treating with 100 μM tetracycline solution conditioned at pH 5. The best quenching was obtained when the probe (N-CDvaline) was prepared by heating l- valine with ethylene glycol at 170 °C for 10 min (Fig. S1a, Supporting Information). All further studies were carried out with the optimized batch of N- CDvaline. The probe solution was prepared in deionized water and the pH of the solution was about 7 (Fig. S1b, Supporting Information).

For real sample analysis, an elementary pre-treatment was performed to avoid the matrix effect in municipality tap water, river water and milk samples.
(a)
Spike analysis of municipality tap water and river water samples

The municipality tap water samples were taken from our laboratory (Roorkee, India) whereas the river water samples were collected from Ganga Canal, Roorkee, India. Prior to spiking both the samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane and centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 6 min to avoid any suspended particles. After that, the above samples were spiked with tetracycline with concentrations ranging between 0.5 and 10.0 μM. The pH and other conditions mentioned above were kept same.
(b)
Spike analysis of milk samples

Milk powder was procured from local market. Elementary pre-treatment of the as-obtained aqueous milk sample was performed to eliminate the proteins, fats and other organic substances [36]. Briefly, 10 g of milk powder was dissolved in 100 mL of DI water, to which trichloroacetic acid (10% w/v) was added and sonicated for 30 min. Then the mixture was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 6 min to separate the precipitate. The supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm membrane (Millipore). Finally, the filtrate was spiked with known concentration of tetracycline solution ranging between 5 μM and 100 μM.

The functional groups in the transverse carbon chains of N-CDvaline is corroborated by the FT-IR spectrum of N-CDvaline (Fig. 4 ). The broad peak in the region 3050 to 3600 cm−1 is attributable to the stretching vibration of O–H group involved in the hydrogen bonding. The peaks at 2956 cm−1 and 2890 cm−1 correspond to symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the C–H bond in the methylene group, respectively. The peak at 1459 cm−1 is due to the bending vibrational mode of –CH2 bond, which corresponded to the oligomerization of ethylene glycol. The peak at 1640 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of C═O in amide bond. The peak at 1365 cm−1 indicates symmetric stretching of the COO− group, while the peaks at 1203 cm−1, 1076 cm−1 and 1042 cm−1, corresponds to the stretching vibration of –C–OH bond, C–C–N stretching mode and C–O–C symmetric stretching vibrations, respectively. The FT-IR studies corroborated the proposed structure of N-CDvaline as depicted in Scheme-1a.

The chemical composition of N-CDvaline is reflected from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies. The full scan survey spectrum of indicates the major constituents e.g., C, N and O (Fig. 5 a). The atomic ratio of C:O: N in N-CDvaline corresponded to 7:3:1. The higher resolution XPS spectrum of the core electrons of C 1s shows a broad peak with at least two shoulder peaks (Fig. 5b). It is de-convoluted into four peaks corresponding to 284.6, 285.2, 286.1 and 288.7 eV, which are attributable to C═C, C–C/C–H, C–O/C–N, O═C–O bonds, respectively. Similarly, the higher resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s exhibited a broad peak, which is deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.5, 532.2 and 533.8 eV (Fig. 5c), which correspond to the C═O, C–O–C and C–OH groups in N-CDvaline respectively. Similarly, the higher resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s shows a broad binding energy peak, which is deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.8 eV (smaller intensity) and a significantly larger intensity peaks at 401.5 eV (Fig. 5d) that correspond to C–N–C and N–H bonds, respectively. Our XPS results of N-CDvaline are consistent with those reported from various types of nitrogen doped carbon dots [39–41].

The mass spectrum offered useful information about the tentative structure of N-CDvaline, which revealed a large number of peaks with m/Z ranging between 149.02077 and 1350.88609 (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The possible molecular structure of most of these peaks are listed in Table S1 (Supporting Information). Considering the m/z ratio from mass spectrum along with NMR and FT-IR studies, two types of transverse carbon chains are predicted: (a) a long chain oligomer of ethylene glycol terminated by l-valine unit and an alkene or an alcohol at either ends; (b) smaller unit of oligomers of ethylene glycol linked by hydrogen bond to form a larger transverse carbon chain, terminated as described in ‘a’. Eventually, these proposed transverse carbon chains are proposed to be laterally connected by hydrogen bonding and by π-π interactions to form a possible non-graphitic N-CDvaline, as represented in Scheme 1a.

The yellowish brown colored clear aqueous solution of N-CDvaline under normal light, exhibited bluish emission in a dark UV chamber illuminated with UV light of λ = 365 nm (inset of Fig. 6 a). The UV–Vis spectrum of N-CDvaline revealed a typical broad spectrum with absorbance peaks at λ = 261 nm and 306 nm (Fig. 6a), corresponding to π–π* transitions and n-π* transition of the amide bond, respectively. The photoluminescence spectra of the N-CDvaline were recorded over a wide range of excitation wavelength (given as supporting Information, Fig. S3). The maximum photoluminescence (PL) intensity was observed at λem = 445 nm when the N-CDvaline is excited at wavelength λex = 370 nm (Fig. 6b). The excitation wavelength dependence PL behavior is commonly reported in nitrogen doped carbon dots, which could be attributable to presence of trap states/defect states, dangling bonds [42] and surface functionalization [43,44]. The relative photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of N-CDvaline was determined as 11.4%, which is nearly similar to those reported for doped non-graphitic carbon dots [45–47]. The optical property of N-CDvaline was further studied by time resolved PL decay measurement and the corresponding PL decay curve is given in (Fig. 6c). The decay curve was best fitted by the tri-exponential function, given as:
(2)
Y(t) = A
1
exp(-t/τ
1
) + A
2
exp(-t/τ
2
)+ A
3
exp(-t/τ
3
)
where the normalized PL intensities as a function of time Y(t) is given as a linear combination of fractional coefficients (A
1 , A
2 and A
3 ) of the time resolved decay lifetimes of τ
1 , τ
2 and τ
3 , respectively. The tri-exponential behaviour of the PL decay implied presence of three distinct emissive sites corresponding to carbon core and surface domain in N-CDvaline. The faster lifetime component (τ
1 ) is due to direct e-h recombination, while the longer lifetime (τ
2 and τ
3 ) correspond to prolonged relaxation of excited charge carrier to lower energy levels, which is attributable to N and O functionalization in the carbon dots [48]. The average lifetime was 7.76 ns, calculated from the given equation [3]:
(3)
< τ a v g > =
∑
i = 1
3
A i τ i 2
∑
i = 1
3
A i τ i

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline by N-CDvaline as PL probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The as-synthesized N-CDvaline was explored as a PL probe for quantitative sensing of tetracycline (TC) in different real sample matrices, e.g., municipality tap water, river water and in milk sample. The analytical method has been optimized using spiking method where the PL quenching responses of N-CDvaline treated with real samples spiked with known concentrations of TC. The PL quenching is given as a ratio of I
o /I, where I o is the photoluminescence intensity of the probe solution and I correspond to the PL intensity due to quenching by tetracycline. The optimum PL quenching of the probe solution was obtained for the batch of tetracycline solution taken at pH 5 (Fig. S4a, given as Supporting Information). For this study, 4 mL of the tetracycline solution of 50 μM (respective pH) was treated with 100 μL of the as synthesized and purified N-CDvaline. The PL quenching attained equilibrium in 2 min, (Fig. S4b, given as Supporting Information).

Under the optimized conditions the PL quenching of N-CDvaline revealed a concave up response as the TC concentration is increased from 0.25 μM to 200.00 μM (Fig. 7 a). The corresponding PL spectra of the probe treated with increasing concentrations of TC are given in Fig. 7b. A good linear fit of the PL quenching was obtained for concentration range between 0.25 and 5.00 μM (inset of Fig. 7a), using the conventional Stern-Volmer equation given as:
I O
I
= 1 +
K
S V
[
T C
]
Here, K sv is Stern Volmer quenching constant and [TC] correspond to the concentration of the quencher i.e. tetracycline. In the plot of I o/I vs [TC], the term KSV is obtained from the slope which also correspond to the sensitivity of the probe towards detection of TC. The sensitivity was determined as 0.082 μM−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was determined as 0.054 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined using the formula:
L O D =
3 σ
S l o p e
o f
l i n e a r
r e g i o n
o f
s t a n d a r d
c a l i b r a t i o n
c u r v e
Where σ = standard deviation of 5 blank measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spike analysis of tetracycline in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: A linear Stern-Volmer based PL quenching of N-CDvaline was observed when treated with municipality tap water and river water samples spiked with TC concentration in the range of 0.5 and 10.0 μM (Fig. 8 a). The sensitivity for TC detection in tap water and river water samples were 0.033 μM−1 and 0.024 μM−1. The corresponding LODs were 0.754 μM and 0.814 μM respectively. The recovery analyses of spiked TC concentration were in the range of 93–110% for tap water samples and 98–126% for river water samples (Table 1 a). The cases with more than 100% recovery was found for the batches spiked with very low TC concentration, which could be due to interference in the PL quenching by the matrix constituents of the samples.

In the case of pre-treated milk samples spiked with 5–100 μM TC, (Fig. 8b) the PL quenching exhibited linear Stern-Volmer based PL quenching response (Fig. 8c). The sensitivity was determined as 0.027 μM−1 and the LOD as 0.92 μM. The recoveries were in the range between 88.7 and 99.4% (Table 1 b). There are very few studies on TC detection in milk samples. Compared to these studies, the TC detection in milk samples by our probe (N-CDvaline) were found to be better in terms of LoD and sensitivity.

The selectivity towards TC detection has been investigated with respect to a wide range of interfering agents, such as metal ions (e.g., Na+, K+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Fe2+), anions (e.g., Cl−, F−, HCO3 −, NO3 −, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, CH3COO−, AsO4 3−) and antibiotics (e.g., erythromycin, penicillin, ciprofloxacin, chloramphenicol, amoxicillin and oxytetracycline). The concentrations of each interfering agent were taken as 50 μM, which was same as that of TC, and their PL quenching studies were performed under the optimized condition. The solutions of the interfering metal ions were prepared by the serial dilution of standard elemental stock solution (for ICP-MS analysis) in deionized water. The solutions of anions were prepared from the respective sodium salts of the corresponding anions. The aqueous solutions of interfering antibiotics were prepared by extraction from their respective tablets. The extracted species were confirmed by comparing with the UV–Vis spectra of the pure samples. The PL spectra of the probe, probe + TC and probe + respective interfering agents are given as Fig. 9 a, b and 9c, respectively. The corresponding plots of I
o
/I for the cations, anions and antibiotic are presented in Fig. 9d, e and 9f, respectively. Most of these species did not interfere in TC detection as reflected from the unchanged I o/I ratios (i.e., 1.0), except for those treated with Fe2+ and Zn2+ which exhibited about 8 and 12% interference. This is also evident from the corresponding increase in the I o/I values for the batches of (probe + TC + Fe2+) and (probe + TC + Zn2+) as compared to the I o/I values of the batches of (probe + TC + other interfering agents). In the case of interference by antibiotics, the I o/I of penicillin and amoxicillin were 1.10, while the PL quenching by structurally similar oxytetracycline was 1.37. These PL quenching values were significantly less than that of tetracycline (I o/I = 3.25), which implied that the N-CDvaline can selectively detect tetracycline and its structurally similar antibiotics by PL quenching method.

The concave up type of PL quenching curve (shown in Fig. 7a) is indicative of a diffusion encounter based collisional mechanism [49,50]. On the other hand, the UV–vis spectra of the batches of N-CDvaline treated with TC exhibited a shift in the absorbance peak towards longer wavelength, which are evident for the batches of the probe treated with TC concentrations more than 30 μM (Fig. 10 a). In addition, Fig. 10a also revealed new absorbance peak for the batches of N-CDvaline treated with higher concentrations of TC (>30 μM). These observations typically indicate formation of complexes due to binding of TC with N-CDvaline probe, via the polar functional groups in both the analyte and the probe. The possible complex formation is corroborated by comparing the average lifetime times of the photoluminescence of the probe and those of probe treated with different concentrations of tetracycline. Their photoluminescence lifetime spectra are given as Supporting Information in Fig. S5. Similar to the probe, the decay curves of the batches of probe treated with different concentrations of tetracycline were also best fitted by tri-exponential decay components (τ1, τ2, τ3). The individual lifetime parameters, their relative intensities and average photoluminescence lifetime of probe treated with analytes are presented in Table 2 . The average lifetimes of probe treated with tetracycline (τ) were similar to that of the probe (τ0), i.e., τ0/τ was nearly unity (see Table 2), which is typically attributed to formation of ground state complex between the probe and the analyte.

In addition, the inner filter effect (IFE) can also contribute to PL quenching due to overlap of PL excitation and emission spectra of N-CDvaline with the absorption peak of the analyte (i.e., TC), as exhibited in Fig. 10b [51,52]. The IFE contribution [53–57] towards PL quenching (i.e. the % IFE) was determined as given below [58,59]:
%
I F E =
[
E
o b s −
E
c o r r
E
o b s
]
× 100
Where,
E
o b s
=
[
I
o b s ,
N − C D
v a l i n e
−
I
o b s
, N − C D
v a l i n e + T C
]
I
o b s ,
N − C D
v a l i n e
E
c o r r
=
[
I
c o r r ,
N − C D
v a l i n e
−
I
c o r r
,
N − C D
v a l i n e + T C
]
I
c o r r ,
N − C D
v a l i n e
I
c o r r =
I
o b s
×
10
[
A
e x
+
A
e m
]
2
Where,
A
e x
and
A
e m
correspond to the absorbance of N-CDvaline after addition of TC at excitation wavelength
λ
e x
= 370
n m
and maximum emission wavelength
λ
e m
= 445
n m
, respectively, I obs is the observed PL intensity, Icorr is the corrected PL intensity after removing IFE from Iobs. Accordingly, the inner filter effect for PL quenching of N-CDvaline by TC was calculated as 42%. A consolidated overview on limit of detection (LoDs) of tetracycline by PL quenching of the nitrogen doped carbon dots, methods of synthesis using different precursors and detection mechanisms are listed in Table 3 . Notably the LoD obtained in the present study is significantly better than the most reported ones. The PL quenching in most of the reported studies are based on IFE, while the PL quenching mechanism in our study is based on both ground state complex formation and inner filter effect. It may be surmised that the PL quenching of N-CDvaline by TC is due to combination of IFE, collisional impact and ground state complex formation between the probe and the analyte.

A simple and rapid method has been developed for synthesizing a non-graphitic nitrogen doped carbon dot as photoluminescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of tetracycline. The detection is based on PL quenching, which satisfied the linear Stern-Volmer equation for a concentration range of 0.25 and 5.00 μM. The limit of detection (0.054 μM) is much less than the minimum residual levels specified by European Union and by USFDA. Its high specificity towards detection of tetracycline over the host of interfering agents is very attractive for translating the method for real sample analysis, as demonstrated by spike analysis in river water and in milk samples with excellent recovery results. Possible quenching mechanism is discussed in the light of inner filter effect and with the ground state complexation phenomena.

Krishanan Bisht: Investigation, Synthesis, Characterization, Formal analysis, Writing . Raj Kumar Dutta: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Conceptualization, Supervision, Interpretation of results, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: KB is grateful to IIT Roorkee for providing a Senior Research Fellowship. RKD is grateful to UGC-DAE-CSR, Kolkata Centre, India for financial support (Grant No. UGC-DAE-CSR-KC/CRS/19/TE09/1077/1093). Authors acknowledge the access of the 300 KV TEM facility at Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, IIT Roorkee. Authors are also thankful to the Institute Instrumentation Centre of IIT Roorkee for XPS and FLS facility.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An inner filter effect based sensor of tetracycline hydrochloride as developed by loading photoluminescent carbon nanodots in the electrospun nanofibers
